request.question: The soundtrack to 'Dancer in the Dark' is by what Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ?

title: Björk
Where Is the Line
Dancer in the Dark
List of songs recorded by Björk
I've Seen It All
Jófríður Ákadóttir
Greta Salóme Stefánsdóttir
Innocence (Björk song)
Michael Flessas
Selmasongs
sentences: Björk Guðmundsdóttir (] , born 21 November 1965), known as Björk ( ), is an Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ.

Over her four-decade career, she has developed an eclectic musical style that draws on a wide range of influences and genres spanning electronic, pop, experimental, classical, trip hop, IDM, and avant-garde styles.
She initially became known as the lead singer of the alternative rock band The Sugarcubes, whose 1987 single "Birthday" was a hit on US and UK indie stations and a favorite among music critics.

Björk embarked on a solo career in 1993, coming to prominence as a solo artist with albums such as "Debut" (1993), "Post" (1995), and "Homogenic" (1997), while collaborating with a range of artists and exploring a variety of multimedia projects.
"Where Is the Line" is a song recorded by Icelandic singer Björk for her sixth studio album "Medúlla".
It was written by the singer herself and co-produced with longtime collaborator Mark Bell.

The track was originally intended to be released as the album's third commercial single, but its release was cancelled just before Björk released the "Drawing Restraint 9" soundtrack, in order to focus all promotional efforts on the film and soundtrack instead.
Some promotional white labels vinyls were released around 2005.
A music video for the song was made by Icelandic Artist Gabríela Friðriksdóttir.
Upon the cancellation of the single, the video was used as part of a video installation project by Friðriksdóttir.
Dancer in the Dark (Danish: Danser i mørket) is a 2000 Danish musical drama film directed by Lars von Trier.

It stars Icelandic singer Björk as a daydreaming immigrant factory worker who suffers from a degenerative eye condition and is saving up to pay for an operation to prevent her young son from suffering the same fate.
Catherine Deneuve, David Morse, Cara Seymour, Peter Stormare, Siobhan Fallon Hogan, and Joel Grey also star.
Icelandic singer and songwriter Björk has recorded more than one hundred songs for eight studio albums, two soundtrack albums, a compilation album, five remix albums and two collaboration albums.
She is the sole writer and producer of most of the songs included in her albums.
She also sometimes plays instruments during her recording sessions.
Moreover, she has provided credited and uncredited contributions on songs recorded by other artists, including background vocals, songwriting, remixing and production.
"I've Seen It All" is a song by Icelandic singer Björk, with lyrics by Sjón and Lars von Trier.
It was released as the first promotional single from the "Dancer in the Dark" soundtrack, "".
The song features vocals from Radiohead frontman Thom Yorke.
It was nominated for an Academy Award for Best Original Song.
Lyrically, it speaks of one coming to terms with the fact that they are going blind.
Jófríður Ákadóttir, known professionally as JFDR, is an Icelandic singer, songwriter and multi-instrumentalist born in 1994.
She is known for her solo project JFDR as well as her work with Samaris, Pascal Pinon and Gangly and works closely with producer and multi-instrumentalist .
Her father is Icelandic composer and musician Áki Ásgeirsson.
Greta Salóme Stefánsdóttir (] ; born 11 November 1986), known outside Iceland simply as Greta Salóme (] ), is an Icelandic singer, songwriter, and violinist in the Icelandic Symphony Orchestra.
She represented Iceland in the Eurovision Song Contest 2012 in Baku, Azerbaijan, in a duet with singer Jónsi with the song "Never Forget".
They qualified from the first semi-final into the Eurovision final and eventually finished in 20th place with 46 points.
She represented Iceland again, this time as a soloist, in the Eurovision Song Contest 2016 with the song "Hear Them Calling", where she failed to qualify.
"Innocence" is a song written and recorded by Icelandic singer Björk.
The song was released as the second single from her seventh full-length studio album, "Volta".
It is co-produced by record producer Timbaland and his protégé Danja.
The song was premiered on Björk's MySpace profile on March 19, 2007 as part of the fan contest to submit ideas for the music video.
The single was released exclusively as a digital download on July 23, 2007.
Michael C. Flessas (born June 2, 1959 in Miami, Florida), is the birth name of American actor Michael Flessas, who is of Greek ancestry.
Flessas' most notable film role was "Angry Man" in the Cannes Film Festival 2000 Palme d'Or winning film "Dancer in the Dark" directed by Danish film director Lars von Trier.
Originally, the director himself considered playing the role but, instead, the role was given to Flessas.
"Dancer in the Dark" starred Icelandic singer/actress Björk who won the Best Actress award at Cannes for her role.

French film icon, César Award winner, and Academy Award nominee Catherine Deneuve, and other noteworthy artists such as Academy Award and Tony Award winner Joel Grey, Peter Stormare, David Morse, and Stellan Skarsgård also performed in the multiple prize winning film.
One of Björk's songs for the film received an Academy Award nomination for Best Song.
Selmasongs: Music from the Motion Picture Soundtrack 'Dancer in the Dark' is the first soundtrack album by Icelandic musician Björk.
It was released on September 18, 2000, by One Little Indian Records to promote and accompany the film "Dancer in the Dark".
In the film Björk starred as Selma Ježková, a Czech immigrant who has moved to the United States.
The album features classical arrangements, as well as melodies and beats composed of sounds from mundane objects, such as factory machines and trains.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: The soundtrack to 'Dancer in the Dark' is by what Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ?
hop1_docs: Mary J. Blige | Mary Jane Blige ( ; born January 11, 1971) is an American singer, songwriter, rapper, model, record producer and actress.  Starting her career as a backing singer on Uptown Records in 1989, Blige released her first album, "What's the 411? ", in 1992, and has released 13 studio albums since and made over 150 guest appearances on other albums and soundtracks.

Selmasongs | Selmasongs: Music from the Motion Picture Soundtrack 'Dancer in the Dark' is the first soundtrack album by Icelandic musician Björk.  It was released on September 18, 2000, by One Little Indian Records to promote and accompany the film "Dancer in the Dark".  In the film Björk starred as Selma Ježková, a Czech immigrant who has moved to the United States.  The album features classical arrangements, as well as melodies and beats composed of sounds from mundane objects, such as factory machines and trains.

Samurai Champloo Music Record: Departure | Samurai Champloo Music Record: Departure is a soundtrack album, released on June 23, 2004, for the anime series "Samurai Champloo".  It is the second of four soundtrack albums released for the show.  The album was produced by Japanese DJ/producer Nujabes, and American MC/producer Fat Jon, and featured Japanese rapper Shing02 and Japanese singer-songwriter MINMI on the album's first and last tracks, respectively.
List of awards and nominations received by Anahí | Anahí is a Mexican singer-songwriter, actress, record producer and dancer.

Vespertine | Vespertine is the fourth solo album by Icelandic musician Björk, released on 27 August 2001, on One Little Indian Records.  It was recorded at locations in Spain, Iceland, and the United States in 2000; production began during the filming of "Dancer in the Dark", which was characterized by conflict between the singer and director Lars von Trier. " Björk", a self-titled coffee table book containing photographs of the singer throughout her career, was released simultaneously with the album.  "Vespertine"' s sound reflected Björk's newly found interest in the music of artists such as Opiate and Console, who were also enlisted as producers.
Charlotte Devaney | Charlotte Devaney is a British DJ, producer, singer, dancer and actress.

Howard Donald | Howard Paul Donald (born 28 April 1968), is an English singer, songwriter, drummer, pianist, dancer, DJ and record producer.  He is a member of English pop-group Take That and a judge on the German reality talent show "Got to Dance".

response.summary_1: Evidence: "'Selmasongs: Music from the Motion Picture Soundtrack 'Dancer in the Dark' is the first soundtrack album by Icelandic musician Björk' (released September 18, 2000)" and "'In the film Björk starred as Selma Ježková'." Bridge/missing fact: the passages identify Björk as the soundtrack artist but do not explicitly list her occupations as "singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ." Next retrieval direction: fetch Björk's main biography/infobox or an authoritative biography page to confirm that her listed occupations include those five roles.
response.hop2_query: Björk biography infobox occupations singer songwriter actress record producer DJ
hop2_docs: Björk | Björk Guðmundsdóttir (] , born 21 November 1965), known as Björk ( ), is an Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ.  Over her four-decade career, she has developed an eclectic musical style that draws on a wide range of influences and genres spanning electronic, pop, experimental, classical, trip hop, IDM, and avant-garde styles.  She initially became known as the lead singer of the alternative rock band The Sugarcubes, whose 1987 single "Birthday" was a hit on US and UK indie stations and a favorite among music critics.  Björk embarked on a solo career in 1993, coming to prominence as a solo artist with albums such as "Debut" (1993), "Post" (1995), and "Homogenic" (1997), while collaborating with a range of artists and exploring a variety of multimedia projects.

When Björk Met Attenborough | When Björk Met Attenborough is a 2013 documentary television film directed by Louise Hooper, executive produced by Lucas Ochoa and produced by Caroline Page.  It was aired for the first time on 27 July 2013 on Channel 4, in conjunction with Pulse Films and One Little Indian Records.  Partly filmed at the Natural History Museum in London, the documentary features an encounter between Icelandic singer-songwriter and musician Björk and English broadcaster and naturalist David Attenborough.  They discuss the nature of music and the intersection between music, nature and technology.  It also follows the singer as she prepares for her Biophilia Tour, along with rehearsal and studio recording.

Stonemilker | "Stonemilker" is a song recorded by Icelandic singer/songwriter Björk for her eighth studio album, "Vulnicura", in 2015.  It was written and produced by Björk with beats by Venezuelan musician Arca.  "Stonemilker" is "Vulnicura"' s opening track, and the first of a six-part narrative that details the devastating end of Björk's relationship to American contemporary artist Matthew Barney.  Björk wrote the lyrics on the same Icelandic beach where the innovative 360-degree music video was later shot.

You Have to Believe | "You Have to Believe" is a 2015 electronic dance song produced by American DJ/remixer/producer Dave Audé, featuring vocals from Australian singer/songwriter/actress Olivia Newton-John and her daughter, singer/actress Chloe Lattanzi, who co-wrote the single with another Australian singer/songwriter, Vassy.

List of songs recorded by Björk | Icelandic singer and songwriter Björk has recorded more than one hundred songs for eight studio albums, two soundtrack albums, a compilation album, five remix albums and two collaboration albums.  She is the sole writer and producer of most of the songs included in her albums.  She also sometimes plays instruments during her recording sessions.  Moreover, she has provided credited and uncredited contributions on songs recorded by other artists, including background vocals, songwriting, remixing and production.
DJ Layla | DJ Layla, born in Chișinău, Moldavian SSR, Soviet Union) is a Moldovan DJ, record producer, singer and songwriter.

Rebecca Scheja | Ania Rebecca Scheja (born 26 January 1989) is a Swedish actress, disc jockey (DJ), singer, songwriter and record producer.  She is the daughter of Staffan and Marianne Scheja and sister of Leonard Scheja.  As a student she attended the Adolf Fredrik's Music School in Stockholm.
hop2_titles: List of songs recorded by Björk
response.summary_2: The soundtrack to Dancer in the Dark ("Selmasongs") is by Icelandic musician Björk. Passage [1] explicitly identifies Björk (Björk Guðmundsdóttir) as an Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ.
